title: Garlic Naan Tandoor Bread
description: Savor the irresistible aroma and soft, pillowy texture of authentic Garlic Naan, baked traditionally in a tandoor. Each bite offers a harmonious blend of pungent garlic, fresh coriander, and the slight tang of yogurt-leavened dough, creating a truly beloved Indian bread. It's perfect for scooping up rich curries or enjoying on its own.

instructions.title: Activate the Yeast
instructions.text: In a small bowl, combine the warm water, granulated sugar, and active dry yeast. Stir gently and let it sit for 5-10 minutes until the mixture becomes frothy and bubbly, indicating the yeast is active. This is crucial for a well-risen naan.

instructions.title: Prepare the Dough
instructions.text: In a large mixing bowl, combine the all-purpose flour and salt. Make a well in the center and pour in the activated yeast mixture, plain yogurt, and 1 tablespoon of the melted ghee. Mix with a spoon or your hands until a shaggy dough forms.

instructions.title: Knead and First Proof
instructions.text: Transfer the dough to a lightly floured surface and knead for 8-10 minutes until it becomes smooth, elastic, and no longer sticky. Form the dough into a ball, lightly grease the bowl with a little ghee, place the dough ball in it, and turn to coat. Cover the bowl with a clean kitchen towel or plastic wrap and let it rise in a warm place for 1.5 to 2 hours, or until doubled in size.

instructions.title: Prepare Garlic-Coriander Topping
instructions.text: While the dough is proofing, in a small bowl, combine the finely minced fresh garlic and finely chopped fresh coriander. Set aside. Prepare your tandoor or high-heat oven (with a pizza stone or cast iron pan) by preheating it to its highest setting (around 500-550°F / 260-290°C).

instructions.title: Shape the Naan and Apply Topping
instructions.text: Gently punch down the risen dough and divide it into 6 equal portions. On a lightly floured surface, take one portion and roll it out into an oval or tear-drop shape, about 6-8 inches long and 1/4 inch thick. Sprinkle a generous amount of the garlic-coriander mixture over one side of the naan, pressing it gently into the dough. If using, sprinkle a few kalonji seeds as well.

instructions.title: Cook in Tandoor and Finish
instructions.text: Carefully lift the naan. Moisten the plain side (without the garlic topping) with a little water – this helps it stick to the tandoor wall. Slap the moistened side onto the inside wall of the preheated tandoor. Cook for 2-3 minutes, or until bubbles appear on the surface and the naan is lightly charred in spots. Using tongs or a tandoor hook, carefully remove the naan. Brush immediately with melted ghee or butter. Repeat with the remaining dough portions. Serve hot.

tips: **Dough Consistency is Key:** The dough should be soft and pliable, not stiff. Adjust water or flour slightly if needed. Over-kneading can make it tough, under-kneading won't develop gluten.
**High Heat is Essential:** For that authentic tandoor-like puff and char, your cooking surface (tandoor, oven stone, or cast iron pan) must be extremely hot. Preheat thoroughly.
**The Water Trick:** Brushing water on the back of the naan before placing it in the tandoor helps it stick and cook evenly.
**Fresh Ingredients:** Always use fresh garlic and coriander for the best flavor and aroma.
**Serve Immediately:** Naan is best enjoyed fresh off the tandoor, brushed with ghee, to experience its soft texture and rich flavor.
article:
## History of Garlic Naan Tandoor Bread

Naan, an iconic leavened flatbread, traces its origins back to ancient Persia and Central Asia, eventually making its way to India with the arrival of the Mughals. It quickly became a staple in royal kitchens, cooked in the intense heat of clay ovens known as tandoors. Originally, naan was a luxurious bread, consumed by royalty and the elite, made with maida (refined flour), yeast, and often enriched with milk or yogurt. Over centuries, its popularity spread, becoming a beloved accompaniment to meals across India. The addition of garlic is a more contemporary, yet universally cherished, innovation that elevates the classic naan, infusing it with a pungent, aromatic flavor that perfectly complements the rich and diverse gravies of Indian cuisine. Garlic Naan represents a delightful evolution of a timeless culinary tradition.

## Why This Recipe Works

This recipe for Garlic Naan is a testament to the power of traditional Indian baking techniques combined with simple, quality ingredients. The inclusion of yogurt in the dough not only adds a characteristic tang but also contributes to the naan's incredibly soft and tender texture, thanks to its lactic acid content. Yeast provides the essential leavening, creating those desirable air pockets and a slight chewiness. Cooking in a tandoor, or a high-heat home oven simulation, ensures rapid cooking at extreme temperatures. This intense heat causes the dough to puff up dramatically, resulting in a light, airy interior and a beautifully charred, crispy exterior. The fresh minced garlic and coriander, pressed into the surface, infuse every bite with an aromatic burst, making it an irresistible culinary experience.

## Chef Tips for Perfection

Achieving perfect Garlic Naan requires attention to detail. Firstly, ensure your yeast is active; a frothy mixture after 5-10 minutes is a good sign. The dough should be soft and slightly sticky after initial kneading, becoming smooth and elastic after 8-10 minutes of dedicated kneading. Do not rush the proofing process; a full 1.5-2 hours allows for proper flavor development and rise. When shaping, avoid using too much flour, which can dry out the naan. Most importantly, the cooking surface must be scorching hot. If using a home oven, preheat your pizza stone or cast iron pan for at least 30-45 minutes at the highest temperature. The quick, high-heat bake is what gives naan its characteristic texture and subtle char. Finally, generously brush with melted ghee immediately after cooking for a luscious finish.

## Serving Suggestions

Garlic Naan is incredibly versatile and pairs wonderfully with a vast array of Indian dishes. Its soft texture and robust flavor make it an ideal accompaniment for rich, creamy curries like Paneer Butter Masala, Chicken Tikka Masala, or Dal Makhani. It's also fantastic for scooping up hearty lentil stews (dals) or soaking up the gravies of vegetable preparations. For a lighter meal, serve it alongside a fresh raita (yogurt dip) or a tangy pickle. It can even be enjoyed simply with a smear of extra ghee and a sprinkle of chaat masala. Consider tearing it into pieces and using it to mop up the last delicious drops of a flavorful sauce – a truly authentic Indian dining experience.

recipeIngredient: 1/2 cup Plain full-fat yogurt (at room temperature)
4 tbsp Ghee or unsalted butter (melted, for brushing)
